content:
The symbols O$, *, \Delta$, and $\square$ are to be filled, one in each box, as shown below.

The rules for filling in the four symbols are as follows.

1) Every row and every column must contain each of the four symbols.

2) Every 2 × 2 square delineated by bold lines must contain each of the four symbols.

Which symbol will occupy the box marked with ‘?’ in the partially filled figure?

options.content: O
$\Delta$
$\square$

explanation:
The rules for filling in the four symbols : O$, *, \Delta$, and $\square$ are as follows :

  1. Every row and every column must contain each of the 4 symbols.

  2. Every $2 \times 2$ square delineated by bold lines must contains each of the 4 symbols.

In the given figure there are 4 rows $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ and 4 columns are $C_1, C_2, C_3$ and $C_4$.

In the row $R_4$ symbol $\square$ will be placed in free space and in column $C_4$ symbol $\square$ will be placed in free space.

Now, is column $C_3$ we can place symbol $O$ in free space and in row $R_2 \Delta$ can be places.

Now, is column $C_2$ we can have to place symbol $*$ symbol in place of ?, as if can not be use in $R_1 C_1$, symbol $*$ is already placed in $R_4 C_1$.

After placing symbols at $R_3 C_1, R_3 C_2$ and $R_1 C_1$ the figure will look alike.

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: A person was born on the fifth Monday of February in a particular year. Which one of the following statements is correct based on the above information?

options.content: The 2<sup>nd</sup> February of that year is a Tuesday
There will be five Sundays in the month of February in that year
The 1<sup>st</sup> February of that year is a Sunday
All Mondays of February in that year have even dates

explanation:
Given that a person was born on the fifth Monday of February in a particular year, it logically infers :

February had 29 days (indicating it was a leap year).

The arrangement of days places five Mondays in the month.

To determine the correct statement :

1. Fifth Monday occurs in February of a leap year :

In a leap year, February has 29 days, meaning it had to be a year divisible by 4 and the month layout should align to produce five Mondays.

2. Total number of days and specific positions :

Since there are five Mondays, the Mondays must fall on the 1st, 8th, 15th, 22nd, and 29th.

Hence, if :

The 1st of February was a Monday, we continue our logic :

2nd February would be a Tuesday.

The 3rd February would be a Wednesday.

Therefore, given the placement of Mondays as calculated above, the precise conclusion is :

$$\textbf{Option A : The 2}^{\text{nd}} \text{February of that year is a Tuesday}$$.

content: Which one of the groups given below can be assembled to get the shape that is shown above using each piece only once without overlapping with each other? (rotation and translation operations may be used).

Four girls P, Q, R and S are studying languages in a University. P is learning French and Dutch. Q is learning Chinese and Japanese. R is learning Spanish and French. S is learning Dutch and Japanese.

Given that: French is easier than Dutch; Chinese is harder than Japanese; Dutch is easier than Japanese, and Spanish is easier than French.

Based on the above information, which girl is learning the most difficult pair of languages?

options.content: P
Q
R
S

explanation:
To determine which girl is learning the most difficult pair of languages, we need to consider the relative difficulty of each language based on the given information:

1. French is easier than Dutch.

  1. Chinese is harder than Japanese.

  2. Dutch is easier than Japanese.

  3. Spanish is easier than French.

Let's list the languages from easiest to hardest based on these guidelines:

  • Spanish

  • French

  • Dutch

  • Japanese

  • Chinese

Now, let's examine the pair of languages each girl is learning and their difficulty levels:

P: French and Dutch

  • French (easier than Dutch)
  • Dutch

Q: Chinese and Japanese

  • Chinese (hardest)
  • Japanese

R: Spanish and French

  • Spanish (easiest)
  • French

S: Dutch and Japanese

  • Dutch
  • Japanese (harder than Dutch)

Comparing all pairs:

P: French and Dutch (Moderate difficulty)

Q: Chinese and Japanese (Hardest pair since both languages are among the most difficult)

R: Spanish and French (Easiest pair)

S: Dutch and Japanese (Moderate to high difficulty)

Clearly, Q is learning the most difficult pair of languages, Chinese and Japanese.

Answer: Option B (Q)

content: If HIDE and CAGE are coded as 19-23-7-11 and 5-2-17-11 respectively, then what is the code for HIGH ?
explanation:
We can see each letter is coded by the prime in its alphabet position:

• A→2 (1st prime)

• B→3 (2nd prime)

• C→5 (3rd prime)

• D→7 (4th prime)

• E→11 (5th prime)

• F→13 (6th prime)

• G→17 (7th prime)

• H→19 (8th prime)

• I→23 (9th prime)

So for HIGH:

H → 19

I → 23

G → 17

H → 19

Code: 19-23-17-19

Answer: Option D.

content:
The given figure is reflected about the horizontal dashed line and then rotated clockwise by $90^{\circ}$ about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the figure.

Which one of the following options correctly shows the resultant figure?

Note: The figures shown are representative.
explanation:
Reflection about the Horizontal dashed line

(i) Reflection inverts the image vertically

(ii) The top part moves to the bottom and vice - versa

(iii) The left and right direction remain unchanged

Rotation by $90^{\circ}$ clockwise

(i) After reflection, the entire figure is rotated clockwise by $90^{\circ}$

(ii) The elements shift positions accordingly

After performing both transformations, the correct figure matches option (B)

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: Which one of the following options has the correct sequence of objects arranged in the increasing number of mirror lines (lines of symmetry)?

options.content: Circle; Square; Equilateral triangle; Isosceles triangle
Isosceles triangle; Equilateral triangle; Square; Circle
Equilateral triangle; Isosceles triangle; Square; Circle
Isosceles triangle; Square; Equilateral triangle; Circle

explanation:
Here’s the count of mirror‐lines for each figure:

• Isosceles triangle: 1

• Equilateral triangle: 3

• Square: 4

• Circle: infinitely many

Arranging in increasing order gives

Isosceles triangle → Equilateral triangle → Square → Circle

That matches Option B.

content: The bar chart gives the batting averages of VK and RS for 11 calendar years from 2012 to 2022. Considering that 2015 and 2019 are world cup years, which one of the following options is true?

options.content: RS has a higher yearly batting average than that of VK in every world cup year.
VK has a higher yearly batting average than that of RS in every world cup year.
VK’s yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of RS between the two world cup years.
RS’s yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of VK in the last three years.

content:
A planar rectangular paper has two V-shaped pieces attached as shown below.

This piece of paper is folded to make the following closed three-dimensional object.

The number of folds required to form the above object is

In a recently held parent-teacher meeting, the teachers had very few complaints about Ravi. After all, Ravi was a hardworking and kind student. Incidentally, almost all of Ravi’s friends at school were hardworking and kind too. But the teachers drew attention to Ravi’s complete lack of interest in sports. The teachers believed that, along with some of his friends who showed similar disinterest in sports, Ravi needed to engage in some sports for his overall development.

Based only on the information provided above, which one of the following statements can be logically inferred with certainty?

options.content: All of Ravi’s friends are hardworking and kind.
No one who is not a friend of Ravi is hardworking and kind.
None of Ravi’s friends are interested in sports.
Some of Ravi’s friends are hardworking and kind.

explanation:
Based on the information provided in the passage, we need to determine which statement can be logically inferred with certainty.

Let's analyze each option:

Option A: All of Ravi’s friends are hardworking and kind.

The passage states that "almost all of Ravi’s friends at school were hardworking and kind," indicating that there may be some exceptions. Therefore, it cannot be inferred with certainty that all of Ravi's friends are hardworking and kind.

Option B: No one who is not a friend of Ravi is hardworking and kind.

The passage does not provide information about other students who are not friends with Ravi. Therefore, we cannot conclude with certainty that no one outside Ravi's friends group is hardworking and kind.

Option C: None of Ravi’s friends are interested in sports.

The passage mentions that "along with some of his friends who showed similar disinterest in sports," indicating that not all of Ravi's friends are disinterested in sports. Hence, this statement cannot be inferred with certainty.

Option D: Some of Ravi’s friends are hardworking and kind.

The passage clearly states that "almost all of Ravi’s friends at school were hardworking and kind," which includes the subset "some." Therefore, it can be logically inferred with certainty that some of Ravi's friends are hardworking and kind.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option D: Some of Ravi’s friends are hardworking and kind.

content: An opaque pyramid (shown below), with a square base and isosceles faces, is suspended in the path of a parallel beam of light, such that its shadow is cast on a screen oriented perpendicular to the direction of the light beam. The pyramid can be reoriented in any direction within the light beam. Under these conditions, which one of the shadows P, Q, R, and S is NOT possible?

options.content: P
Q
R
S

explanation:
An opaque pyramid with a square base and isosceles faces is suspended in the path of a parallel beam of light such that its shadow is cast an a screen oriented perpendicular to the direction of the light beam. The pyramid can be reoriented in any direction within the light beam under these conditions the shadow Q , is not possible.

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content:
Four cities P, Q, R and S are connected through one-way routes as shown in the figure. The travel time between any two connected cities is one hour. The boxes beside each city name describe the starting time of first train of the day and their frequency of operation. For example, from city P, the first trains of the day start at 8 AM with a frequency of 90 minutes to each of R and S. A person does not spend additional time at any city other than the waiting time for the next connecting train.

If the person starts from R at 7 AM and is required to visit S and return to R, what is the minimum time required?

options.content: 6 hours 30 minutes
3 hours 45 minutes
4 hours 30 minutes
5 hours 15 minutes

explanation:
The travel time between any two cities is 1 hour.

The total journey time is calculated as follows :

R to Q : 1 hour travel time

Wait at Q : 1 hour

Q to P : 1 hour travel time

Wait at P : 1 hour

P to S : 1 hour travel time

Wait at S : 30 minutes

S to R : 1 hour travel time

Therefore, the total journey time from R back to R is 6 hours and 30 minutes.

Thus, the correct option is (A).

content:
Humans are naturally compassionate and honest. In a study using strategically placed wallets that appear “lost”, it was found that wallets with money are more likely to be returned than wallets without money. Similarly, wallets that had a key and money are more likely to be returned than wallets with the same amount of money alone. This suggests that the primary reason for this behavior is compassion and empathy.

Which one of the following is the CORRECT logical inference based on the information in the above passage?

options.content: Wallets with a key are more likely to be returned because people do not care about money
Wallets with a key are more likely to be returned because people relate to suffering of others
Wallets used in experiments are more likely to be returned than wallets that are really lost
Money is always more important than keys

explanation:
The passage suggests that wallets with money and wallets with money and a key have a higher likelihood of being returned compared to wallets without these items. The reason given is based on compassion and empathy.

Here's an analysis of each option :

Option A : This option states, "Wallets with a key are more likely to be returned because people do not care about money." According to the passage, this is incorrect because it mentions that wallets with money are more likely to be returned than wallets without money, indicating that people do care about money.

Option B : This option states, "Wallets with a key are more likely to be returned because people relate to suffering of others." This aligns with the information in the passage, which suggests the behavior is driven by compassion and empathy, hence relating to the suffering or inconvenience of the wallet’s owner.

Option C : This option states, "Wallets used in experiments are more likely to be returned than wallets that are really lost." The passage does not provide any information about the likelihood of wallets being returned in experiments versus real-life situations, so this option cannot be inferred from the passage.

Option D : This option states, "Money is always more important than keys." The passage does not suggest this, as it actually notes that wallets with both a key and money are more likely to be returned than wallets with the same amount of money alone, indicating the additional importance of keys.

Therefore, the correct logical inference based on the information in the passage is :

Option B : Wallets with a key are more likely to be returned because people relate to suffering of others.

content:
Given below are three conclusions drawn based on the following three statements.

Statement 1 : All teachers are professors.

Statement 2 : No professor is a male.

Statement 3 : Some males are engineers.

Conclusion I : No engineer is a professor.

Conclusion II : Some engineers are professors.

Conclusion III : No male is a teacher.

Which one of the following options can be logically inferred?

options.content: Only conclusion III is correct
Only conclusion I and conclusion II are correct
Only conclusion II and conclusion III are correct
Only conclusion I and conclusion III are correct

explanation:
Let's determine which of the conclusions can be logically inferred :

Conclusion I : No engineer is a professor

Given the information, it is unclear if some engineers are professors. Therefore, both possibilities—some engineers being professors or no engineers being professors—remain valid. Thus, this cannot be conclusively inferred.

Conclusion II : Some engineers are professors

Similar to Conclusion I, there is no clear information indicating if some engineers are professors. Therefore, this conclusion is also undetermined and cannot be conclusively inferred.

Conclusion III : No male is a teacher

This can be inferred. As illustrated in the diagram, all teachers are professors, and no professor is male. Consequently, no male can be a teacher.

Hence, the correct option is (A).
